claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex who I'm responsible for.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask a few questions about Alex to determine your eligibility.

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 16, I need to ask:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying for A-levels, doing a college course, or an approved training program)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He left school and isn't studying anything right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask:

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I need to ask a few more questions about your circumstances:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex's living situation:

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions:

**Is Alex currently in paid work for 24 hours or more a week?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

**Is Alex claiming any benefits in his own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits himself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, **I'm afraid you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While Child Benefit normally stops when a child turns 16, it can continue if the child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training. Since Alex has left education and is not in any approved training, this condition isn't met.

However, there is a **20-week grace period** available if Alex registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. This means that if Alex were to register with careers services, you could potentially claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks while he's looking for work or deciding his next steps.

**My recommendation:** It would be worth encouraging Alex to register with a careers service, as this could:
- Make you eligible for Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks
- Provide Alex with support in finding work, training, or further education opportunities

If Alex does register, or if his circumstances change (such as starting education, training, or an apprenticeship), please contact the Child Benefit Office as you may then become eligible.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left education. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, while Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19, this only applies if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Additionally, when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, but Alex has not registered with either. Since Alex does not meet the criteria for continuing Child Benefit after age 16 (not in approved education/training and not registered with careers services), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 16-year-old son Alex. While the basic residential requirements are met, Alex has left education and is not in approved training, nor has he registered with careers services or the armed services. Under Child Benefit rules, payments for 16-17 year olds require the child to be in approved education/training or registered with careers services within the 20-week grace period. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Since Alex does not meet the criteria for continuing Child Benefit after age 16 (not in approved education/training and not registered with careers services), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 16-year-old son Alex. While the basic residential requirements are met, Alex has left education and is not in approved training, nor has he registered with careers services or the armed services. Under Child Benefit rules, payments for 16-17 year olds require the child to be in approved education/training or registered with careers services within the 20-week grace period. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
